nodes.en: The science of substances and their transformations: composition, structure, bonding, and the reactions by which matter changes. Spans general, organic, inorganic, physical, analytical, environmental, medicinal, materials and computational chemistry plus biochemistry; distinguished from Physics by its focus on elements, compounds and reactions rather than fundamental forces.
nodes.cs: Věda o látkách a jejich přeměnách: složení, struktura, vazby a reakce, jimiž se hmota mění. Zahrnuje obecnou, organickou, anorganickou, fyzikální, analytickou, environmentální, medicinální, materiálovou a výpočetní chemii a biochemii; od fyziky se liší zaměřením na prvky, sloučeniny a reakce, nikoli na fundamentální síly.
nodes.brief:
Chemistry is the science of substances and their transformations: the composition, structure and bonding of matter, and the reactions by which one substance becomes another. It works at the scale of atoms and molecules, building from the elements up through compounds, mixtures and materials, and asks not only what a substance is but how and why it reacts the way it does.

Chemistry sits beside [[01.01]] within the Natural Sciences: physics supplies the fundamental forces and quantum rules that govern bonding, while chemistry applies them to the specific behavior of elements and compounds — the same electromagnetic interaction underlies both, but chemistry treats it at the scale of the reaction rather than the field. Its subfields divide along where the emphasis falls: [[01.02.02]] specializes in carbon-based compounds and the reactions that build living matter, [[01.02.06]] extends organic chemistry into the molecules and pathways of the cell, and [[01.02.09]] applies the same bonding principles to the design of solids with engineered properties.

Chemistry borders [[01.03]] most directly through biochemistry, where the chemical reactions of metabolism, genetics and cell signaling are chemistry's own subject matter studied inside a living system. For durable reference material, see the [IUPAC Gold Book](https://goldbook.iupac.org/) of chemical terminology and the [Encyclopaedia Britannica entry on chemistry](https://www.britannica.com/science/chemistry).

nodes.en: The shared foundations of chemistry: atomic structure, the periodic table, chemical bonding, stoichiometry, states of matter, solutions, acid–base and redox chemistry, and basic equilibrium and kinetics. The entry layer whose topics the specialized sibling branches deepen.
nodes.cs: Společné základy chemie: stavba atomu, periodická tabulka, chemická vazba, stechiometrie, skupenství, roztoky, acidobazická a redoxní chemie a základy chemické rovnováhy a kinetiky. Vstupní vrstva, jejíž témata specializované sourozenecké obory dále prohlubují.
nodes.brief:
General Chemistry lays out the shared vocabulary and machinery the rest of the field builds on: atomic structure and the periodic table, the nature of chemical bonding, stoichiometry, the states of matter and solutions, and the basic behavior of acid–base and redox reactions together with elementary equilibrium and kinetics. It is the entry layer of [[01.02]] — every specialized branch assumes these foundations rather than re-deriving them.

Its material feeds directly into the specialized siblings: [[01.02.02]] and [[01.02.03]] apply the same bonding and periodic trends to carbon-based and non-carbon systems respectively, while [[01.02.04]] deepens the thermodynamic and kinetic theory that General Chemistry introduces only at an introductory level. Where those branches specialize by substance class or by underlying physics, General Chemistry stays deliberately broad, covering a little of everything a working chemist needs before choosing a direction.

For a durable reference, the freely available [OpenStax Chemistry](https://openstax.org/details/books/chemistry-2e) textbook and the [IUPAC Gold Book](https://goldbook.iupac.org/) of standardized terminology anchor the field's core vocabulary.

nodes.en: The chemistry of carbon compounds: functional groups, nomenclature, stereochemistry, reaction mechanisms and organic synthesis, from hydrocarbons to complex natural products. Carbon chemistry inside living organisms is Biochemistry's domain.
nodes.cs: Chemie sloučenin uhlíku: funkční skupiny, názvosloví, stereochemie, reakční mechanismy a organická syntéza, od uhlovodíků po složité přírodní látky. Chemií uhlíku v živých organismech se zabývá biochemie.
nodes.brief:
Organic Chemistry studies the compounds of carbon: functional groups and nomenclature, stereochemistry, reaction mechanisms, and the synthesis of molecules ranging from simple hydrocarbons to complex natural products. It builds directly on the bonding and reactivity principles [[01.02.01]] introduces, specializing them to carbon's unique capacity for chains, rings and stereocenters.

Its boundary with [[01.02.03]] runs along the carbon skeleton: once a compound's chemistry is dominated by a metal center or a non-carbon framework, it falls to Inorganic Chemistry instead. Organic Chemistry also stops at the edge of the living cell — the same carbon compounds studied as metabolic intermediates, enzyme substrates or genetic material belong to [[01.02.06]], while Organic Chemistry treats carbon reactivity in the abstract, independent of biological context. [[01.02.08]] draws on organic synthesis and mechanism to design bioactive molecules, turning general synthetic methods toward specific drug targets.

For canonical reference, see the [IUPAC nomenclature recommendations](https://iupac.qmul.ac.uk/) for organic compounds and the widely used open-access [LibreTexts Organic Chemistry](https://chem.libretexts.org/Bookshelves/Organic_Chemistry) textbook collection.

nodes.en: Chemistry of the elements outside the carbon-skeleton domain: main-group and transition-metal chemistry, coordination compounds, organometallics, solid-state structures and industrial inorganic processes.
nodes.cs: Chemie prvků mimo doménu uhlíkatých skeletů: chemie hlavních skupin a přechodných kovů, koordinační sloučeniny, organokovové látky, struktury pevných látek a průmyslové anorganické procesy.
nodes.brief:
Inorganic Chemistry covers everything chemistry outside the carbon-skeleton domain: main-group and transition-metal chemistry, coordination compounds, organometallic chemistry, solid-state structures, and the industrial processes built on them. Where [[01.02.02]] specializes in carbon's chain- and ring-forming chemistry, Inorganic Chemistry ranges across the rest of the periodic table, including compounds — organometallics — that contain carbon but are governed by a metal center rather than by carbon chemistry itself.

It shares its theoretical grounding with [[01.02.04]], drawing on crystal-field and ligand-field theory to explain the color, magnetism and reactivity of coordination compounds, and it feeds forward into [[01.02.09]], where inorganic solid-state structures — ceramics, semiconductors, catalytic surfaces — are engineered for specific material properties.

For reference, the [IUPAC Red Book](https://iupac.org/what-we-do/books/nomenclature-of-inorganic-chemistry/) recommendations for inorganic nomenclature and the [Royal Society of Chemistry's inorganic chemistry hub](https://www.rsc.org/journals-books-databases/subject/inorganic-chemistry/) document the field's core terminology and current research.

nodes.en: The physics underlying chemical behaviour: chemical thermodynamics, kinetics, quantum chemistry, the theory of spectroscopy and electrochemistry. Explains why reactions occur and how fast; measurement techniques for determining composition belong to Analytical Chemistry.
nodes.cs: Fyzika stojící za chemickým chováním: chemická termodynamika, kinetika, kvantová chemie, teorie spektroskopie a elektrochemie. Vysvětluje, proč reakce probíhají a jak rychle; měřicí techniky pro určování složení patří do analytické chemie.
nodes.brief:
Physical Chemistry supplies the physics underlying chemical behavior: chemical thermodynamics and equilibrium, reaction kinetics, quantum chemistry, the theoretical basis of spectroscopy, and electrochemistry. It explains why reactions occur and how fast, treating a chemical system the way [[01.01]] treats any physical system — through energy, force and quantum-mechanical structure — but applied specifically to bonds, molecules and reacting mixtures.

It divides from [[01.02.05]] along the line between explanation and measurement: Physical Chemistry supplies the theory of why a spectrum or a titration curve looks the way it does, while Analytical Chemistry turns that theory into the instruments and protocols that determine what a sample actually contains. [[01.02.10]] extends Physical Chemistry's quantum-mechanical and statistical-mechanical models into numerical simulation, using the computer to solve equations Physical Chemistry poses analytically only for the simplest cases.

For canonical treatment, see [Atkins' Physical Chemistry](https://global.oup.com/academic/product/atkins-physical-chemistry-9780198919157) and the [IUPAC Green Book](https://iupac.org/what-we-do/books/greenbook/) of physicochemical quantities and units.

nodes.en: Determining what a sample contains and in what amount: separation methods such as chromatography and extraction, spectroscopic and mass-spectrometric identification, electroanalysis, and calibration, validation and uncertainty in quantitative measurement.
nodes.cs: Určování, co vzorek obsahuje a v jakém množství: separační metody jako chromatografie a extrakce, spektroskopická a hmotnostně-spektrometrická identifikace, elektroanalýza a kalibrace, validace a nejistoty kvantitativního měření.
nodes.brief:
Analytical Chemistry determines what a sample contains and in what amount: separation techniques such as chromatography and extraction, spectroscopic and mass-spectrometric identification, electroanalytical methods, and the calibration, validation and uncertainty analysis that make a quantitative result trustworthy.

It takes the theory [[01.02.04]] supplies — why a compound absorbs light at a given wavelength, why an ion migrates at a given rate — and turns it into repeatable measurement protocols; the two fields split along the line between explaining a phenomenon and using it to quantify a sample. That measurement capability is put to direct use downstream: [[01.02.07]] relies on analytical methods to monitor pollutant concentrations in air, water and soil, and drug development pipelines lean on the same techniques to verify purity and dosage.

For methodology reference, see the [IUPAC analytical chemistry division](https://iupac.org/body/500/) recommendations and the open-access [LibreTexts Analytical Chemistry](https://chem.libretexts.org/Bookshelves/Analytical_Chemistry) textbook collection.

nodes.en: The chemistry of life: proteins, nucleic acids, carbohydrates and lipids, enzyme catalysis, metabolic pathways and molecular energetics. Sits at the chemistry–biology boundary — the chemical mechanisms of living processes, as opposed to Organic Chemistry's general carbon chemistry.
nodes.cs: Chemie života: proteiny, nukleové kyseliny, sacharidy a lipidy, enzymová katalýza, metabolické dráhy a molekulární energetika. Leží na pomezí chemie a biologie — chemické mechanismy životních procesů, na rozdíl od obecné chemie uhlíku v organické chemii.
nodes.brief:
Biochemistry studies the chemistry of life: proteins, nucleic acids, carbohydrates and lipids, the catalytic mechanisms of enzymes, metabolic pathways, and the molecular energetics that power a cell. It sits at the boundary between chemistry and biology — the same carbon-based molecules [[01.02.02]] studies in the abstract, here examined specifically as they function inside a living system.

Where Organic Chemistry treats a molecule's reactivity independent of context, Biochemistry asks how that reactivity is harnessed by enzymes, regulated by metabolic control, and integrated into cellular signaling — its natural cross-branch neighbor is [[01.03]], where the same molecules are studied at the level of the organism rather than the reaction. [[01.02.08]] draws on Biochemistry's account of enzymes and receptors to identify the molecular targets a drug is designed against.

For canonical reference, see [Lehninger's Principles of Biochemistry](https://www.macmillanlearning.com/college/us/product/Lehninger-Principles-of-Biochemistry/p/1319228003) and the open-access [NCBI Biochemistry bookshelf](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK21154/).

nodes.en: Chemical processes in air, water and soil: pollutant sources, transport and fate, atmospheric chemistry including ozone and greenhouse gases, water-treatment chemistry, and the monitoring and remediation of contamination.
nodes.cs: Chemické procesy v ovzduší, vodě a půdě: zdroje, transport a osud znečišťujících látek, chemie atmosféry včetně ozonu a skleníkových plynů, chemie úpravy vody a monitorování a sanace kontaminace.
nodes.brief:
Environmental Chemistry studies chemical processes in air, water and soil: the sources, transport and fate of pollutants, atmospheric chemistry including ozone depletion and greenhouse-gas cycling, the chemistry behind water treatment, and the monitoring and remediation of contamination.

It depends on [[01.02.05]] for the measurement techniques that detect pollutants at trace concentrations, and on the reaction kinetics [[01.02.04]] supplies to model how quickly a pollutant transforms or degrades once released. Its atmospheric and aqueous reactions often involve the same inorganic species — nitrogen and sulfur oxides, metal ions, ozone — that [[01.02.03]] studies in isolation, but Environmental Chemistry follows them through an open natural system rather than a controlled one.

For reference, see the [US EPA's environmental chemistry resources](https://www.epa.gov/environmental-topics) and the [Royal Society of Chemistry's environmental chemistry hub](https://www.rsc.org/journals-books-databases/subject/environmental-science-and-technology/).

nodes.en: Design and optimization of bioactive molecules into drugs: structure–activity relationships, pharmacophores, lead discovery and optimization, ADMET properties and drug–target interactions. Chemistry-driven drug design — the clinical use of drugs belongs to the medical and pharmacology branches.
nodes.cs: Návrh a optimalizace bioaktivních molekul na léčiva: vztahy mezi strukturou a účinkem, farmakofory, hledání a optimalizace vodítek (lead compounds), vlastnosti ADMET a interakce léčivo–cíl. Chemicky vedený návrh léčiv — klinické užití léčiv patří do medicínských a farmakologických větví.
nodes.brief:
Medicinal Chemistry designs and optimizes bioactive molecules into drugs: structure–activity relationships, pharmacophore identification, lead discovery and optimization, and the ADMET properties (absorption, distribution, metabolism, excretion, toxicity) that determine whether a promising molecule survives to become a medicine.

It is chemistry-driven drug design specifically — the synthetic routes it optimizes are [[01.02.02]]'s methods turned toward a therapeutic target, and the targets themselves — enzymes, receptors, ion channels — are the molecules [[01.02.06]] describes in their normal cellular role. Increasingly, [[01.02.10]] contributes virtual screening and molecular docking that narrow a candidate list before a single compound is synthesized. The clinical use, dosing and prescribing of the resulting drugs belongs to medicine and pharmacology rather than to Medicinal Chemistry itself.

For reference, see the [FDA's drug development process overview](https://www.fda.gov/patients/drug-development-process) and the [Royal Society of Chemistry's medicinal chemistry hub](https://www.rsc.org/journals-books-databases/subject/medicinal-chemistry/).

nodes.en: Synthesis and chemical design of functional materials: polymers, ceramics, nanomaterials, semiconductors, catalysts and coatings, and the structure–property relationships that tailor them for applications in energy, electronics and construction.
nodes.cs: Syntéza a chemický návrh funkčních materiálů: polymery, keramika, nanomateriály, polovodiče, katalyzátory a povlaky a vztahy mezi strukturou a vlastnostmi, které je přizpůsobují aplikacím v energetice, elektronice a stavebnictví.
nodes.brief:
Materials Chemistry synthesizes and chemically designs functional materials: polymers, ceramics, nanomaterials, semiconductors, catalysts and coatings, tailored through structure–property relationships for applications in energy, electronics and construction.

It draws its solid-state and coordination chemistry from [[01.02.03]] and its macromolecular, polymer-forming chemistry from [[01.02.02]], combining both toward a material's engineered function rather than toward understanding a single reaction or compound in isolation. [[01.02.10]] increasingly guides that design in advance, simulating a candidate material's electronic or mechanical properties before it is ever synthesized.

For reference, see the [Materials Project](https://materialsproject.org/) open database of computed material properties and the [Royal Society of Chemistry's materials chemistry hub](https://www.rsc.org/journals-books-databases/subject/materials-science/).

nodes.en: The branch of chemistry that uses computer simulation and numerical methods — quantum-mechanical calculations, molecular dynamics, and modeling — to predict molecular structures, reaction energetics, and properties. Distinct from Physical Chemistry (which supplies the underlying theory) and Analytical Chemistry (physical measurement): here the computer is the instrument.
nodes.cs: Odvětví chemie, které pomocí počítačových simulací a numerických metod — kvantověmechanických výpočtů, molekulové dynamiky a modelování — předpovídá struktury molekul, energetiku reakcí a vlastnosti látek. Na rozdíl od fyzikální chemie (jež dodává teorii) a analytické chemie (fyzické měření) je zde nástrojem počítač.
nodes.brief:
Computational Chemistry uses computer simulation and numerical methods — quantum-mechanical calculations, molecular dynamics, and statistical modeling — to predict molecular structures, reaction energetics and material properties without first running the experiment.

It solves numerically the equations [[01.02.04]] poses analytically only for the simplest systems, and it is often discussed alongside [[01.02.05]] because both determine a molecule's properties — the difference is that here the computer, not an instrument, is what does the determining. Its predictions feed forward into practice: [[01.02.09]] uses computed electronic and mechanical properties to screen candidate materials before synthesis, and [[01.02.08]] uses molecular docking and virtual screening to narrow drug candidates before they are made.

For reference, see the [NIST Computational Chemistry Comparison and Benchmark Database](https://cccbdb.nist.gov/) and the open-source [Psi4 quantum chemistry package](https://psicode.org/) documentation.

nodes.name: Chemical Disciplines
nodes.en: The chemical sciences organized by discipline — from organic, inorganic and physical chemistry through analytical, materials, electro-, photo- and computational chemistry to the nuclear, environmental, medicinal and supramolecular frontiers. A deep authoritative map of chemistry's branches, their core theory, and their active research fronts.
nodes.cs: Chemické vědy uspořádané podle disciplín — od organické, anorganické a fyzikální chemie přes analytickou, materiálovou, elektro-, foto- a výpočetní chemii až po jaderné, environmentální, medicinální a supramolekulární pomezí. Hloubková autoritativní mapa odvětví chemie, jejich základní teorie a aktivních výzkumných front.

nodes.name: Organic Chemistry
nodes.en: Organic chemistry is the study of the structure, reactivity, and synthesis of carbon-based compounds, encompassing functional-group transformations, reaction mechanisms, stereochemistry, and multistep synthetic strategy. It spans from bonding fundamentals through pericyclic, radical, and organometallic chemistry to the total synthesis of complex natural products and pharmaceuticals, distinguishing it from Inorganic Chemistry's non-carbon systems and Biochemistry's focus on biological macromolecules.
nodes.cs: Organická chemie je studium struktury, reaktivity a syntézy sloučenin na bázi uhlíku, zahrnující přeměny funkčních skupin, reakční mechanismy, stereochemii a strategie vícekrokové syntézy. Sahá od základů vazby přes pericyklickou, radikálovou a organokovovou chemii až po totální syntézu komplexních přírodních látek a léčiv, čímž se odlišuje od anorganické chemie zaměřené na sloučeniny bez uhlíku a biochemie zaměřené na biologické makromolekuly.

nodes.name: Structure, Bonding & Stereochemistry
nodes.en: Covers the electronic and three-dimensional basis of carbon compounds: hybridization, resonance, conformational analysis, and the classification of chirality, configuration, and molecular symmetry. This foundation underlies every prediction of reactivity, selectivity, and physical property elsewhere in organic chemistry. Settled since the mid-20th century but continually refined by computational and X-ray methods.
nodes.brief:
Covers the electronic and three-dimensional basis of carbon compounds: hybridization, resonance, conformational analysis, and the classification of chirality, configuration, and molecular symmetry. This foundation underlies every prediction of reactivity, selectivity, and physical property elsewhere in organic chemistry. Settled since the mid-20th century but continually refined by computational and X-ray methods.

### Concepts
- **Hybridization & Resonance** — Molecular orbital mixing (sp3/sp2/sp) and delocalized electron distribution explain bond geometry and stability.
- **Conformational Analysis** — Rotation about single bonds generates distinct energy minima (e.g. chair cyclohexane) that govern reactivity and stability.
- **Chirality & Stereodescriptors** — R/S and E/Z nomenclature systematically encodes three-dimensional spatial arrangement at stereocenters and double bonds.
- **Conformational & Configurational Isomerism** — Diastereomers, enantiomers, and atropisomers arise from distinct spatial relationships without breaking bonds.
- **Aromaticity** — Cyclic, planar, conjugated systems obeying Huckel's rule gain exceptional thermodynamic stabilization.

nodes.name: Reaction Mechanisms & Physical Organic Chemistry
nodes.en: Studies how and why organic reactions proceed, using kinetics, thermodynamics, linear free-energy relationships, and isotope effects to map transition states and intermediates such as carbocations, carbanions, and radicals. It provides the causal logic connecting substrate structure to reaction outcome. A mature discipline, though computational transition-state modeling keeps sharpening mechanistic detail.
nodes.brief:
Studies how and why organic reactions proceed, using kinetics, thermodynamics, linear free-energy relationships, and isotope effects to map transition states and intermediates such as carbocations, carbanions, and radicals. It provides the causal logic connecting substrate structure to reaction outcome. A mature discipline, though computational transition-state modeling keeps sharpening mechanistic detail.

### Concepts
- **Nucleophilic Substitution & Elimination** — SN1/SN2 and E1/E2 pathways compete based on substrate, nucleophile, solvent, and base strength.
- **Carbocation & Carbanion Chemistry** — Charged reactive intermediates rearrange and are stabilized by hyperconjugation, resonance, or neighboring-group participation.
- **Linear Free-Energy Relationships** — Hammett and related plots quantitatively correlate substituent electronics with reaction rate or equilibrium.
- **Kinetic Isotope Effects** — Rate changes upon isotopic substitution reveal which bonds break in the rate-determining step.
- **Acid-Base & Catalytic Mechanisms** — Bronsted and Lewis acid-base catalysis lower activation barriers by stabilizing developing charge in the transition state.

nodes.name: Functional Group Chemistry & Named Reactions
nodes.en: Organizes organic reactivity around characteristic functional groups—alcohols, carbonyls, amines, alkenes, and their derivatives—and the canonical named transformations that interconvert them. This is the workhorse taxonomy taught in every introductory sequence and used daily in synthesis planning. Core reactions are textbook-settled; new variants and greener conditions remain an active refinement area.
nodes.brief:
Organizes organic reactivity around characteristic functional groups—alcohols, carbonyls, amines, alkenes, and their derivatives—and the canonical named transformations that interconvert them. This is the workhorse taxonomy taught in every introductory sequence and used daily in synthesis planning. Core reactions are textbook-settled; new variants and greener conditions remain an active refinement area.

### Concepts
- **Carbonyl Chemistry** — Aldehydes, ketones, and carboxylic acid derivatives undergo nucleophilic addition and acyl substitution at the electrophilic carbon.
- **Enolate & Aldol Chemistry** — Deprotonated carbonyl compounds act as nucleophiles in aldol, Claisen, and Mannich carbon-carbon bond formations.
- **Alkene & Alkyne Reactions** — Addition, oxidation, and metathesis reactions exploit pi-bond nucleophilicity to install new functionality.
- **Named Reactions** — Well-characterized transformations such as Diels-Alder, Wittig, and Grignard addition provide reliable, predictable synthetic outcomes.
- **Protecting Group Strategy** — Temporary functional-group masking enables chemoselective reaction sequences on polyfunctional molecules.

nodes.name: Pericyclic, Radical & Photochemistry
nodes.en: Examines concerted electrocyclic, cycloaddition, and sigmatropic reactions governed by orbital symmetry, alongside chain and non-chain radical processes and light-driven excited-state transformations. Woodward-Hoffmann rules and frontier molecular orbital theory give these seemingly disparate mechanisms a unified predictive framework. Classical pericyclic theory is settled; synthetic radical and photoredox methodology are fast-moving frontiers.
nodes.brief:
Examines concerted electrocyclic, cycloaddition, and sigmatropic reactions governed by orbital symmetry, alongside chain and non-chain radical processes and light-driven excited-state transformations. Woodward-Hoffmann rules and frontier molecular orbital theory give these seemingly disparate mechanisms a unified predictive framework. Classical pericyclic theory is settled; synthetic radical and photoredox methodology are fast-moving frontiers.

### Concepts
- **Woodward-Hoffmann Rules** — Orbital symmetry conservation dictates whether pericyclic reactions proceed thermally or photochemically, and suprafacially or antarafacially.
- **Cycloadditions & Electrocyclizations** — Diels-Alder-type and ring-closing pericyclic reactions form rings via concerted, single-transition-state pathways.
- **Sigmatropic Rearrangements** — Cope and Claisen rearrangements migrate sigma bonds across a pi system with predictable stereochemical outcome.
- **Radical Chain Reactions** — Initiation, propagation, and termination steps drive halogenation, polymerization, and radical cyclization chemistry.
- **Photoredox Catalysis** — Visible-light-excited photocatalysts generate radical intermediates under mild conditions for selective bond formation.

nodes.name: Organometallic & Catalytic Synthesis
nodes.en: Focuses on carbon-metal bonded reagents and transition-metal catalytic cycles—oxidative addition, transmetalation, reductive elimination—that enable cross-coupling, C-H functionalization, and asymmetric catalysis. This chemistry underpins most modern industrial and pharmaceutical carbon-carbon bond construction. Foundational cross-coupling is mature (Nobel-recognized), while C-H activation and earth-abundant-metal catalysis remain highly active research frontiers.
nodes.brief:
Focuses on carbon-metal bonded reagents and transition-metal catalytic cycles—oxidative addition, transmetalation, reductive elimination—that enable cross-coupling, C-H functionalization, and asymmetric catalysis. This chemistry underpins most modern industrial and pharmaceutical carbon-carbon bond construction. Foundational cross-coupling is mature (Nobel-recognized), while C-H activation and earth-abundant-metal catalysis remain highly active research frontiers.

### Concepts
- **Cross-Coupling Reactions** — Pd- and Ni-catalyzed Suzuki, Negishi, and Heck couplings forge C-C bonds between organohalides and organometallic partners.
- **C-H Activation & Functionalization** — Transition-metal catalysts selectively insert into inert C-H bonds, bypassing prefunctionalization steps.
- **Asymmetric Catalysis** — Chiral metal or organocatalysts induce enantioselectivity in bond-forming reactions, critical for single-enantiomer drug synthesis.
- **Organolithium & Grignard Reagents** — Highly nucleophilic carbanion equivalents add to electrophiles as fundamental carbon-nucleophile building blocks.
- **Metathesis Chemistry** — Ru- and Mo-carbene catalysts redistribute alkene or alkyne substituents to form new unsaturated bonds.

nodes.name: Total Synthesis & Retrosynthetic Strategy
nodes.en: Addresses the design and execution of multistep routes to complex natural products and target molecules, using retrosynthetic analysis to disconnect a target into simpler precursors. It integrates mechanism, stereochemistry, and catalysis into holistic synthetic planning and remains the discipline's showcase of strategic creativity. Classical retrosynthetic logic is settled; efficient, scalable, and computer-assisted route design is an active frontier.
nodes.brief:
Addresses the design and execution of multistep routes to complex natural products and target molecules, using retrosynthetic analysis to disconnect a target into simpler precursors. It integrates mechanism, stereochemistry, and catalysis into holistic synthetic planning and remains the discipline's showcase of strategic creativity. Classical retrosynthetic logic is settled; efficient, scalable, and computer-assisted route design is an active frontier.

### Concepts
- **Retrosynthetic Analysis** — Working backward from a target structure via bond disconnections identifies feasible synthetic precursors and routes.
- **Protecting Group & Chemoselectivity Strategy** — Sequencing reactive-group manipulations avoids unwanted side reactions across a multistep synthesis.
- **Natural Product Total Synthesis** — Complete laboratory construction of complex bioactive molecules validates strategy and often discovers new methodology.
- **Convergent vs Linear Synthesis** — Combining independently built fragments late in a route improves overall yield versus a single sequential chain.
- **Computer-Aided Synthesis Planning** — Machine-learning route-prediction tools propose and rank retrosynthetic disconnections at scale.

nodes.name: Spectroscopy & Structure Elucidation
nodes.en: Encompasses the spectroscopic and analytical techniques—NMR, IR, mass spectrometry, X-ray crystallography—used to determine the connectivity, conformation, and purity of organic compounds. Structure elucidation is the indispensable verification step for every claimed synthesis or mechanism. Core techniques are mature; advances in cryo-EM-scale resolution and automated structure prediction continue to push sensitivity and throughput.
nodes.brief:
Encompasses the spectroscopic and analytical techniques—NMR, IR, mass spectrometry, X-ray crystallography—used to determine the connectivity, conformation, and purity of organic compounds. Structure elucidation is the indispensable verification step for every claimed synthesis or mechanism. Core techniques are mature; advances in cryo-EM-scale resolution and automated structure prediction continue to push sensitivity and throughput.

### Concepts
- **NMR Spectroscopy** — Nuclear spin resonance patterns reveal atom connectivity, stereochemistry, and dynamic conformational behavior.
- **Mass Spectrometry** — Ionization and fragmentation patterns determine molecular weight and substructure of an analyte.
- **Infrared & UV-Vis Spectroscopy** — Vibrational and electronic absorption bands diagnose functional groups and conjugated chromophores.
- **X-Ray Crystallography** — Diffraction of a crystalline sample provides unambiguous three-dimensional atomic-resolution structure.

nodes.name: Modern Synthetic Frontiers
nodes.en: Gathers emerging methodologies reshaping how organic molecules are made and studied: flow and automated synthesis, biocatalysis, click and bioorthogonal chemistry, and computational reaction discovery. These approaches prioritize sustainability, speed, and functional precision beyond classical batch methods. This is the branch's active research edge, with rapid ongoing methodological turnover.
nodes.brief:
Gathers emerging methodologies reshaping how organic molecules are made and studied: flow and automated synthesis, biocatalysis, click and bioorthogonal chemistry, and computational reaction discovery. These approaches prioritize sustainability, speed, and functional precision beyond classical batch methods. This is the branch's active research edge, with rapid ongoing methodological turnover.

### Concepts
- **Flow Chemistry & Automation** — Continuous-flow reactors and robotic platforms enable rapid, reproducible, and scalable reaction optimization.
- **Click & Bioorthogonal Chemistry** — Highly selective, high-yielding reactions like azide-alkyne cycloaddition function cleanly inside living systems.
- **Biocatalysis & Directed Evolution** — Engineered enzymes catalyze organic transformations with exceptional selectivity under mild, sustainable conditions.
- **Green & Sustainable Synthesis** — Solvent reduction, atom economy, and renewable feedstocks minimize the environmental footprint of synthetic routes.
- **Machine-Learning-Guided Reaction Discovery** — Data-driven models predict reaction outcomes and propose novel transformations beyond human-curated rule sets.

nodes.name: Inorganic Chemistry
nodes.en: Inorganic chemistry is the study of the structure, bonding, and reactivity of all elements and compounds outside the carbon-hydrogen skeletons that define organic chemistry, spanning main-group and transition-metal chemistry, coordination compounds, bioinorganic systems, solid-state and cluster chemistry, and the ligand-field theory explaining transition-metal electronic structure. Organic Chemistry's carbon-backbone compounds and Biochemistry's carbon chemistry inside living organisms lie outside its scope except at the bioinorganic interface.
nodes.cs: Anorganická chemie je studium struktury, vazeb a reaktivity všech prvků a sloučenin mimo uhlíkovo-vodíkové kostry definující organickou chemii, zahrnující chemii hlavních skupin a přechodných kovů, koordinační sloučeniny, bioanorganické systémy, chemii pevných látek a klastrů a teorii ligandového pole vysvětlující elektronovou strukturu přechodných kovů. Sloučeniny s uhlíkovou kostrou náležející organické chemii a uhlíková chemie uvnitř živých organismů (biochemie) stojí mimo její záběr, kromě bioanorganického rozhraní.

nodes.name: Main-Group Chemistry
nodes.en: Covers the systematic chemistry of s- and p-block elements — hydrides, oxides, halides, and Zintl-phase compounds — framed by periodic trends in electronegativity, ionization energy, and bond covalency. Explains diagonal relationships, the inert-pair effect, and multiple bonding in heavier main-group elements, underlying low-valent and unusual-oxidation-state main-group species. Foundational to industrial inorganic synthesis and to understanding reactivity trends across the periodic table.
nodes.cs: Zabývá se systematickou chemií prvků s- a p-bloku — hydridy, oxidy, halogenidy a Zintlovými fázemi — v rámci periodických trendů elektronegativity, ionizační energie a kovalence vazby. Vysvětluje diagonální vztahy, efekt inertního páru a vícenásobné vazby u těžších prvků hlavních skupin, jež stojí za nízkovalentními sloučeninami a neobvyklými oxidačními stavy. Je základem průmyslové anorganické syntézy a chápání trendů reaktivity napříč periodickou tabulkou.
nodes.brief:
Covers the systematic chemistry of s- and p-block elements — hydrides, oxides, halides, and Zintl-phase compounds — framed by periodic trends in electronegativity, ionization energy, and bond covalency. Explains diagonal relationships, the inert-pair effect, and multiple bonding in heavier main-group elements, which underlies low-valent and unusual-oxidation-state main-group species. Foundational to industrial inorganic synthesis and to understanding reactivity across the per

### Concepts
- **Periodic Trends** — Systematic variation of atomic/ionic radius, ionization energy, and electronegativity governs main-group reactivity.
- **Hydrides and Oxides** — Binary compounds of main-group elements with hydrogen and oxygen classify by bonding type — ionic, covalent, or metallic.
- **Zintl Phases** — Intermetallic compounds between electropositive and post-transition elements form polyanionic clusters obeying Zintl-Klemm rules.
- **Low-Valent Main-Group Species** — Stabilized carbene-analogue and multiply-bonded compounds of heavier p-block elements challenge classical valence rules.
- **Acid-Base Concepts** — Hard-soft acid-base (HSAB) theory rationalizes main-group Lewis acid-base pairing and reactivity trends.

nodes.name: Coordination Chemistry and Ligand Field Theory
nodes.en: Studies the structure, bonding, and electronic spectra of transition-metal complexes, from Werner's coordination theory through crystal field, ligand field, and molecular orbital treatments of d-orbital splitting. Explains color, magnetism, and stability of complexes via spectrochemical and nephelauxetic series, Jahn-Teller distortion, and spin-crossover behavior. Forms the conceptual core connecting transition-metal structure to reactivity and spectroscopy.
nodes.brief:
Studies the structure, bonding, and electronic spectra of transition-metal complexes, from Werner's coordination theory through crystal field, ligand field, and molecular orbital treatments of d-orbital splitting. Explains color, magnetism, and stability of complexes via spectrochemical and nephelauxetic series, Jahn-Teller distortion, and spin-crossover behavior. Forms the conceptual core connecting transition-metal structure to reactivity and spectroscopy.

### Concepts
- **Crystal and Ligand Field Theory** — d-orbital splitting in a ligand field explains color, magnetism, and geometric preferences of transition-metal complexes.
- **Spectrochemical Series** — Empirical ordering of ligands by their field strength predicts d-d splitting magnitude and complex color.
- **Jahn-Teller Distortion** — Degenerate electronic ground states in octahedral complexes drive geometric distortion to lower symmetry.
- **Magnetochemistry** — Magnetic susceptibility measurements reveal spin state, oxidation state, and metal-metal interactions in coordination compounds.
- **Coordination Isomerism and Stereochemistry** — Geometric and optical isomerism in complexes arises from ligand arrangement around the metal center.

nodes.name: Organometallic Chemistry
nodes.en: Examines compounds with direct metal-carbon bonds across the d- and f-block, governed by the 18-electron rule, hapticity, and back-bonding in metal-carbonyl and metal-alkene systems. Central to homogeneous catalysis — hydrogenation, cross-coupling, olefin polymerization, and C-H activation — bridging inorganic and organic synthesis. A highly active field driving industrial catalyst design and green chemistry.
nodes.brief:
Examines compounds with direct metal-carbon bonds across the d- and f-block, governed by the 18-electron rule, hapticity, and back-bonding in metal-carbonyl and metal-alkene systems. Central to homogeneous catalysis — hydrogenation, cross-coupling, olefin polymerization, and C-H activation — bridging inorganic and organic synthesis. A highly active field driving industrial catalyst design and green chemistry.

### Concepts
- **18-Electron Rule** — Electron-counting formalism predicts stability and reactivity of organometallic complexes.
- **Metal Carbonyls and π-Backbonding** — Synergic σ-donation/π-backbonding between metal d-orbitals and CO π* orbitals stabilizes carbonyl complexes.
- **Catalytic Cycles** — Oxidative addition, migratory insertion, and reductive elimination steps compose fundamental organometallic catalytic mechanisms.
- **C-H Activation** — Direct functionalization of inert C-H bonds via transition-metal complexes enables atom-economical synthesis.
- **Cross-Coupling Catalysis** — Pd- and Ni-catalyzed coupling reactions form carbon-carbon bonds from organohalides and organometallic partners.

nodes.name: Bioinorganic Chemistry
nodes.en: Investigates the roles of metal ions in biological systems, including metalloenzyme active sites, electron-transfer proteins, oxygen transport, and metal-based therapeutics. Applies coordination and spectroscopic principles to understand catalytic mechanisms of enzymes such as cytochrome P450, nitrogenase, and photosystem II. A rapidly growing interface between inorganic chemistry, structural biology, and medicine.
nodes.brief:
Investigates the roles of metal ions in biological systems, including metalloenzyme active sites, electron-transfer proteins, oxygen transport, and metal-based therapeutics. Applies coordination and spectroscopic principles to understand catalytic mechanisms of enzymes such as cytochrome P450, nitrogenase, and photosystem II. A rapidly growing interface between inorganic chemistry, structural biology, and medicine.

### Concepts
- **Metalloenzyme Active Sites** — Metal cofactors in enzymes such as nitrogenase and hydrogenase enable challenging small-molecule transformations.
- **Oxygen Transport Proteins** — Heme and non-heme iron/copper proteins like hemoglobin and hemocyanin reversibly bind and transport dioxygen.
- **Electron-Transfer Metalloproteins** — Iron-sulfur clusters and blue-copper centers mediate long-range biological electron transfer.
- **Medicinal Inorganic Chemistry** — Metal-based drugs such as cisplatin and metal radiopharmaceuticals exploit metal reactivity for therapeutic and diagnostic use.
- **Metal Homeostasis and Transport** — Chaperone proteins and transporters regulate cellular uptake, trafficking, and storage of essential trace metals.

nodes.name: Solid-State and Materials Chemistry
nodes.en: Addresses the structure, bonding, and defect chemistry of extended inorganic solids — oxides, chalcogenides, and framework materials — using band theory and crystallography. Encompasses superconductors, ionic conductors, magnetic materials, and metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), linking structure to electronic, magnetic, and porous functional properties. A major frontier for energy storage, catalysis, and quantum materials research.
nodes.brief:
Addresses the structure, bonding, and defect chemistry of extended inorganic solids — oxides, chalcogenides, and framework materials — using band theory and crystallography. Encompasses superconductors, ionic conductors, magnetic materials, and metal-organic frameworks (MOFs), linking structure to electronic, magnetic, and porous functional properties. A major frontier for energy storage, catalysis, and quantum materials research.

### Concepts
- **Band Theory of Solids** — Delocalized molecular orbital treatment of periodic lattices explains metallic, semiconducting, and insulating behavior.
- **Defect Chemistry** — Point, line, and planar defects in crystalline solids govern ionic conductivity, diffusion, and nonstoichiometry.
- **Metal-Organic Frameworks** — Porous crystalline networks of metal nodes and organic linkers enable gas storage, separation, and catalysis.
- **Superconducting and Quantum Materials** — Cuprate, iron-pnictide, and related inorganic solids exhibit high-temperature superconductivity and correlated-electron phenomena.
- **Solid-State Ionic Conductors** — Fast-ion-conducting oxides and sulfides underpin solid-state batteries and fuel cell electrolytes.

nodes.name: Cluster, Cage, and Polynuclear Chemistry
nodes.en: Covers polynuclear inorganic species including boranes, carboranes, metal-metal bonded clusters, and polyoxometalates, rationalized through Wade-Mingos electron-counting rules and cluster bonding theory. Explores metal-metal multiple bonding and giant molecular architectures with applications in catalysis, magnetism, and nanoscale materials. Bridges molecular and solid-state inorganic chemistry through delocalized cluster bonding.
nodes.brief:
Covers polynuclear inorganic species including boranes, carboranes, metal-metal bonded clusters, and polyoxometalates, rationalized through Wade-Mingos electron-counting rules and cluster bonding theory. Explores metal-metal multiple bonding and giant molecular architectures with applications in catalysis, magnetism, and nanoscale materials. Bridges molecular and solid-state inorganic chemistry through delocalized cluster bonding.

### Concepts
- **Wade-Mingos Rules** — Skeletal electron-pair counting predicts closo, nido, and arachno cluster geometries in boranes and metal clusters.
- **Metal-Metal Multiple Bonds** — Quadruple and higher-order metal-metal bonds in dinuclear complexes exhibit distinctive electronic structure and reactivity.
- **Polyoxometalates** — Anionic clusters of early transition-metal oxo units self-assemble into nanoscale molecular architectures.
- **Boranes and Carboranes** — Electron-deficient boron hydride clusters and their carbon analogues exhibit three-center two-electron bonding.
- **Cluster Catalysis** — Multi-metal cluster cores enable cooperative activation of small molecules unavailable to mononuclear centers.

nodes.name: Inorganic Reaction Mechanisms and Kinetics
nodes.en: Studies the pathways and rates of substitution, redox, and ligand-exchange reactions at metal centers, distinguishing associative, dissociative, and interchange mechanisms. Applies Marcus theory to electron-transfer kinetics and examines trans/cis effects governing substitution selectivity in square-planar and octahedral complexes. Provides the mechanistic foundation for designing catalytic and photochemical inorganic transformations.
nodes.brief:
Studies the pathways and rates of substitution, redox, and ligand-exchange reactions at metal centers, distinguishing associative, dissociative, and interchange mechanisms. Applies Marcus theory to electron-transfer kinetics and examines trans/cis effects governing substitution selectivity in square-planar and octahedral complexes. Provides the mechanistic foundation for designing catalytic and photochemical inorganic transformations.

### Concepts
- **Substitution Mechanisms** — Associative, dissociative, and interchange pathways describe ligand-exchange kinetics at metal centers.
- **Trans and Cis Effects** — Ligand identity kinetically and thermodynamically directs substitution position in square-planar complexes.
- **Electron-Transfer Theory** — Marcus theory relates inner- and outer-sphere electron-transfer rates to reorganization energy and driving force.
- **Redox Mechanisms** — Inner-sphere and outer-sphere pathways distinguish bridged versus non-bonded electron-transfer routes between metal centers.
- **Photochemistry of Complexes** — Light-induced ligand-field and charge-transfer excited states drive photosubstitution and photoredox reactivity.

nodes.name: Lanthanide, Actinide, and Nuclear Inorganic Chemistry
nodes.en: Examines f-block elements, whose contracted 4f/5f orbitals produce characteristic magnetic, optical, and coordination behavior distinct from d-block metals, alongside the specialized chemistry of radioactive actinides. Covers separation science for nuclear fuel cycles, single-molecule magnets, and luminescent lanthanide complexes. An area of intense current interest for nuclear waste management and quantum information applications.
nodes.brief:
Examines f-block elements, whose contracted 4f/5f orbitals produce characteristic magnetic, optical, and coordination behavior distinct from d-block metals, alongside the specialized chemistry of radioactive actinides. Covers separation science for nuclear fuel cycles, single-molecule magnets, and luminescent lanthanide complexes. An area of intense current interest for nuclear waste management and quantum information applications.

### Concepts
- **Lanthanide Contraction** — Poor 4f-orbital shielding causes a steady ionic-radius decrease across the lanthanide series, affecting coordination number.
- **f-Element Luminescence** — Sharp, long-lived emission from lanthanide ions arises from Laporte-forbidden f-f transitions sensitized by antenna ligands.
- **Single-Molecule Magnets** — Lanthanide complexes with strong magnetic anisotropy exhibit slow magnetic relaxation below a blocking temperature.
- **Actinide Separation Chemistry** — Selective ligand extraction methods separate actinides from lanthanides for nuclear fuel reprocessing.
- **Nuclear Fuel Cycle Chemistry** — Coordination and redox chemistry of uranium, plutonium, and minor actinides underpins reactor fuel and waste management.

nodes.name: Physical Chemistry
nodes.en: Physical chemistry applies the quantitative laws of physics to chemical systems, explaining why reactions occur, how fast, and what governs molecular structure and phase behavior. Spans thermodynamics and equilibrium, reaction kinetics, quantum chemistry, statistical mechanics, spectroscopy, and surface and colloid phenomena, providing the theoretical and instrumental toolkit other chemistry disciplines draw on. Distinct from Theoretical & Computational Chemistry's algorithmic focus and from Analytical Chemistry's emphasis on measuring composition rather than explaining mechanism.
nodes.cs: Fyzikální chemie aplikuje kvantitativní zákony fyziky na chemické systémy a vysvětluje, proč reakce probíhají, jak rychle a co určuje molekulární strukturu a fázové chování. Zahrnuje termodynamiku a rovnováhy, reakční kinetiku, kvantovou chemii, statistickou mechaniku, spektroskopii a povrchové a koloidní jevy a poskytuje teoretický a přístrojový aparát, z něhož čerpají ostatní chemické obory. Liší se od Teoretické a výpočetní chemie důrazem na algoritmický přístup a od Analytické chemie, která se soustředí na měření složení, nikoli na vysvětlení mechanismu.

nodes.name: Chemical Thermodynamics & Equilibria
nodes.en: Studies energy, entropy, and free energy transformations that govern whether and how far chemical and physical processes proceed. Establishes the laws of thermodynamics as applied to reactions, phase changes, and mixtures, and predicts equilibrium composition, phase behavior, and work/heat exchange. Underpins virtually every quantitative prediction in chemistry, from reaction feasibility to material stability.
nodes.brief:
Studies energy, entropy, and free energy transformations that govern whether and how far chemical and physical processes proceed. Establishes the laws of thermodynamics as applied to reactions, phase changes, and mixtures, and predicts equilibrium composition, phase behavior, and work/heat exchange. Underpins virtually every quantitative prediction in chemistry, from reaction feasibility to material stability.

### Concepts
- **Laws of Thermodynamics** — The zeroth through third laws set the conservation, directionality, and absolute-zero constraints on energy and entropy.
- **Chemical Potential & Free Energy** — Gibbs and Helmholtz energies quantify the driving force and spontaneity of processes at constant pressure or volume.
- **Phase Equilibria** — Phase diagrams and the Clausius-Clapeyron relation describe conditions under which solid, liquid, and gas phases coexist.
- **Solution Thermodynamics** — Activity coefficients and excess functions describe deviations of real mixtures from ideal-solution behavior.
- **Chemical Equilibrium** — The equilibrium constant relates standard free-energy change to the composition-dependent reaction quotient at balance.
- **Electrochemical Thermodynamics** — The Nernst equation links cell potential to free energy change and ion activities in redox equilibria.

nodes.name: Chemical Kinetics & Reaction Dynamics
nodes.en: Investigates the rates, mechanisms, and molecular-level pathways of chemical transformations, bridging macroscopic rate laws with microscopic collision and energy-transfer events. Covers catalysis, elementary-step mechanisms, and the theoretical frameworks that predict rate constants from molecular structure. Femtosecond spectroscopy and single-molecule methods keep pushing mechanistic resolution toward real-time observation of bond-breaking events.
nodes.brief:
Investigates the rates, mechanisms, and molecular-level pathways of chemical transformations, bridging macroscopic rate laws with microscopic collision and energy-transfer events. Covers catalysis, elementary-step mechanisms, and the theoretical frameworks that predict rate constants from molecular structure. Femtosecond spectroscopy and single-molecule methods keep pushing mechanistic resolution toward real-time observation of bond-breaking events.

### Concepts
- **Rate Laws & Reaction Order** — Empirical rate expressions relate reaction velocity to reactant concentrations and temperature via the Arrhenius equation.
- **Transition State Theory** — Models the reaction rate as passage over an activated complex at a free-energy saddle point along the reaction coordinate.
- **Collision & Molecular Beam Dynamics** — Crossed molecular beam experiments resolve state-to-state reactive scattering cross sections at the single-collision level.
- **Catalysis Kinetics** — Homogeneous and enzyme catalysis mechanisms, including Michaelis-Menten kinetics, describe rate acceleration via alternative pathways.
- **Femtochemistry** — Ultrafast pump-probe spectroscopy tracks transition-state dynamics on the femtosecond timescale of bond formation and cleavage.
- **Non-Equilibrium & Complex Kinetics** — Oscillating reactions and chaotic kinetics reveal far-from-equilibrium dynamical behavior in coupled reaction networks.

nodes.name: Quantum Chemistry & Electronic Structure
nodes.en: Applies quantum mechanics to compute molecular electronic structure, bonding, and properties from first principles. Encompasses wavefunction-based ab initio methods, density functional theory, and semi-empirical approximations used to predict spectra, reactivity, and potential energy surfaces. A mature theoretical core paired with an intensely active computational frontier driven by algorithmic and hardware advances, including quantum computing applications.
nodes.brief:
Applies quantum mechanics to compute molecular electronic structure, bonding, and properties from first principles. Encompasses wavefunction-based ab initio methods, density functional theory, and semi-empirical approximations used to predict spectra, reactivity, and potential energy surfaces. A mature theoretical core paired with an intensely active computational frontier driven by algorithmic and hardware advances, including quantum computing applications.

### Concepts
- **Schrodinger Equation & Orbitals** — The molecular Schrodinger equation, solved approximately via atomic and molecular orbital theory, describes electron distribution.
- **Ab Initio Methods** — Hartree-Fock and post-Hartree-Fock methods (MP2, coupled cluster) systematically approximate the many-electron wavefunction.
- **Density Functional Theory** — DFT recasts electronic structure as a functional of electron density, enabling tractable calculations on larger molecular systems.
- **Potential Energy Surfaces** — Multidimensional energy surfaces map how molecular energy varies with nuclear geometry, defining reaction paths and transition states.
- **Computational Chemistry Software & Basis Sets** — Basis-set expansions and algorithmic implementations translate quantum theory into practical numerical predictions.
- **Quantum Computing for Chemistry** — Emerging quantum algorithms target electronic-structure problems intractable for classical computers at scale.

nodes.name: Statistical Mechanics & Molecular Simulation
nodes.en: Connects microscopic molecular properties to macroscopic thermodynamic observables through ensemble theory and partition functions. Provides the statistical foundation for computing entropy, heat capacity, and equilibrium constants from molecular energy levels, and underlies molecular dynamics and Monte Carlo simulation methods. Classical ensemble theory is settled; simulation methodology for complex and non-equilibrium systems remains a fertile research area.
nodes.brief:
Connects microscopic molecular properties to macroscopic thermodynamic observables through ensemble theory and partition functions. Provides the statistical foundation for computing entropy, heat capacity, and equilibrium constants from molecular energy levels, and underlies molecular dynamics and Monte Carlo simulation methods. Classical ensemble theory is settled; simulation methodology for complex and non-equilibrium systems remains a fertile research area.

### Concepts
- **Statistical Ensembles** — Microcanonical, canonical, and grand canonical ensembles formalize how microstate probability distributions yield bulk thermodynamic properties.
- **Partition Functions** — The partition function encodes all accessible energy states of a system and generates thermodynamic quantities via differentiation.
- **Molecular Dynamics Simulation** — Numerical integration of classical equations of motion propagates atomic trajectories to sample structural and dynamic properties.
- **Monte Carlo Methods** — Stochastic sampling algorithms estimate equilibrium averages over configuration space without solving explicit dynamics.
- **Statistical Thermodynamics of Gases** — Translational, rotational, vibrational, and electronic partition functions predict ideal-gas thermodynamic functions from spectroscopic data.
- **Non-Equilibrium Statistical Mechanics** — Fluctuation theorems and linear response theory extend statistical mechanics to driven, non-equilibrium systems.

nodes.name: Spectroscopy & Molecular Structure Determination
nodes.en: Studies the interaction of electromagnetic radiation with matter to determine molecular structure, dynamics, and composition. Spans rotational, vibrational, and electronic spectroscopy as well as magnetic resonance and diffraction techniques used across chemistry, biology, and materials science. Foundational selection-rule theory is well established, while multidimensional and single-molecule spectroscopic techniques continue to expand resolution and applicability.
nodes.brief:
Studies the interaction of electromagnetic radiation with matter to determine molecular structure, dynamics, and composition. Spans rotational, vibrational, and electronic spectroscopy as well as magnetic resonance and diffraction techniques used across chemistry, biology, and materials science. Foundational selection-rule theory is well established, while multidimensional and single-molecule spectroscopic techniques continue to expand resolution and applicability.

### Concepts
- **Rotational & Vibrational Spectroscopy** — Microwave and infrared spectroscopy probe molecular bond lengths, angles, and vibrational force constants via quantized transitions.
- **Electronic Spectroscopy** — UV-visible absorption and emission spectroscopy reveal electronic transitions and excited-state molecular properties.
- **Nuclear Magnetic Resonance** — NMR exploits nuclear spin transitions in a magnetic field to determine molecular connectivity and three-dimensional structure.
- **Raman Spectroscopy** — Inelastic light scattering probes vibrational modes complementary to infrared, sensitive to polarizability changes.
- **Mass Spectrometry** — Ionization and mass-to-charge analysis determine molecular mass, fragmentation patterns, and structural composition.
- **X-ray & Electron Diffraction** — Diffraction of X-rays or electrons by crystalline or gas-phase samples resolves precise three-dimensional atomic structure.

nodes.name: Surface Chemistry & Colloid Science
nodes.en: Examines phenomena at interfaces between phases, including adsorption, catalysis on surfaces, and the stability of dispersed colloidal systems. Governs behavior critical to heterogeneous catalysis, nanomaterials, emulsions, and biological membranes. Classical adsorption isotherms and colloid stability theory are well established, with nanoscale interfacial engineering as an active growth area.
nodes.brief:
Examines phenomena at interfaces between phases, including adsorption, catalysis on surfaces, and the stability of dispersed colloidal systems. Governs behavior critical to heterogeneous catalysis, nanomaterials, emulsions, and biological membranes. Classical adsorption isotherms and colloid stability theory are well established, with nanoscale interfacial engineering as an active growth area.

### Concepts
- **Adsorption & Surface Isotherms** — Langmuir and BET isotherms quantify how gas or solute molecules bind to solid surfaces as a function of pressure or concentration.
- **Heterogeneous Catalysis** — Reactions occurring at solid catalyst surfaces proceed through adsorption, surface reaction, and desorption steps.
- **Colloidal Stability** — DLVO theory balances van der Waals attraction and electrostatic repulsion to predict aggregation or stability of colloidal particles.
- **Surface Tension & Interfacial Phenomena** — Surface free energy governs wetting, capillarity, and the shape of liquid interfaces described by the Young-Laplace equation.
- **Micelles & Self-Assembly** — Amphiphilic molecules spontaneously organize into micelles, bilayers, and vesicles above a critical concentration threshold.
- **Nanomaterial Interfaces** — Nanoscale surface-to-volume ratios produce size-dependent electronic, optical, and catalytic properties distinct from bulk materials.

nodes.name: Electrochemistry & Transport Phenomena
nodes.en: Covers the physical chemistry of charge transfer at electrodes, ion transport in solution, and diffusion-driven processes that govern batteries, fuel cells, corrosion, and sensors. Integrates thermodynamic and kinetic treatments of redox reactions with mass-transport theory. Core electrode-kinetics theory is mature, while energy-storage electrochemistry remains a rapidly evolving applied frontier.
nodes.brief:
Covers the physical chemistry of charge transfer at electrodes, ion transport in solution, and diffusion-driven processes that govern batteries, fuel cells, corrosion, and sensors. Integrates thermodynamic and kinetic treatments of redox reactions with mass-transport theory. Core electrode-kinetics theory is mature, while energy-storage electrochemistry remains a rapidly evolving applied frontier.

### Concepts
- **Electrode Kinetics** — The Butler-Volmer equation relates current density to overpotential at an electrode-electrolyte interface.
- **Electrochemical Cells & Batteries** — Galvanic and electrolytic cell design principles underlie energy conversion and storage in batteries and fuel cells.
- **Ion Transport & Conductivity** — Ionic mobility and molar conductivity describe how dissolved ions migrate under concentration and electric-field gradients.
- **Diffusion & Mass Transport** — Fick's laws govern the diffusive flux of species across concentration gradients in solution and at interfaces.
- **Corrosion Electrochemistry** — Electrochemical potential differences drive metal oxidation processes that underlie corrosion mechanisms and prevention.

nodes.name: Photochemistry & Photophysics
nodes.en: Studies light-induced electronic excitation and the subsequent chemical or physical relaxation pathways of excited molecular states. Covers photoinduced reactions, luminescence, and energy transfer processes central to solar energy conversion, photocatalysis, and photobiology. Foundational excited-state theory is well developed, while applications in photocatalysis and light-harvesting materials remain an active research frontier.
nodes.brief:
Studies light-induced electronic excitation and the subsequent chemical or physical relaxation pathways of excited molecular states. Covers photoinduced reactions, luminescence, and energy transfer processes central to solar energy conversion, photocatalysis, and photobiology. Foundational excited-state theory is well developed, while applications in photocatalysis and light-harvesting materials remain an active research frontier.

### Concepts
- **Excited-State Relaxation** — Jablonski-diagram pathways of fluorescence, phosphorescence, and internal conversion describe how excited molecules dissipate energy.
- **Photochemical Reactions** — Light absorption promotes molecules into reactive excited states that follow distinct pathways from thermal ground-state chemistry.
- **Photocatalysis** — Light-activated catalysts drive redox chemistry such as water splitting and organic transformations using photon energy.
- **Energy & Electron Transfer** — Forster and Dexter mechanisms describe how excitation energy or electrons migrate between chromophores over defined distances.
- **Solar Energy Conversion** — Photovoltaic and photosynthetic-mimetic systems convert absorbed photon energy into electrical or chemical potential.

nodes.name: Analytical Chemistry
nodes.en: Analytical chemistry identifies, separates, and quantifies the components of matter, combining chromatographic and electroanalytical separations with spectroscopic and mass-spectrometric detection, chemometric data analysis, and sensor-based measurement. It supplies the measurement methodology that the other chemical disciplines rely on to characterize composition, rather than studying reactivity or synthesis itself.
nodes.cs: Analytická chemie identifikuje, odděluje a kvantifikuje složky látek, a to kombinací chromatografických a elektroanalytických separací se spektroskopickou a hmotnostně-spektrometrickou detekcí, chemometrickou analýzou dat a senzorovým měřením. Poskytuje měřicí metodiku, na niž se spoléhají ostatní chemické obory při charakterizaci složení, sama se však nezabývá reaktivitou ani syntézou.

nodes.name: Separation Science
nodes.en: Covers the physicochemical principles and instrumentation used to resolve complex mixtures into individual components prior to detection or quantification. Encompasses gas, liquid, and supercritical-fluid chromatography as well as electrophoretic and electrokinetic separations. Underpins virtually every modern analytical workflow, from pharmaceutical QC to omics profiling.
nodes.brief:
Covers the physicochemical principles and instrumentation used to resolve complex mixtures into individual components prior to detection or quantification. Encompasses gas, liquid, and supercritical-fluid chromatography as well as electrophoretic and electrokinetic separations. Underpins virtually every modern analytical workflow, from pharmaceutical QC to omics profiling.

### Concepts
- **Gas Chromatography** — Separates volatile analytes by differential partitioning between a mobile gas phase and a stationary liquid or solid phase in a column.
- **High-Performance Liquid Chromatography** — Separates non-volatile and thermally labile compounds using a pressurized liquid mobile phase over a packed or monolithic stationary phase.
- **Capillary Electrophoresis** — Separates charged species in narrow capillaries by their differential migration velocities under an applied electric field.
- **Ion Chromatography** — Separates and quantifies inorganic and organic ions using ion-exchange stationary phases coupled with conductivity or suppressed detection.
- **Supercritical Fluid Chromatography** — Uses a supercritical fluid, typically CO2, as mobile phase to combine GC-like efficiency with LC-like selectivity for thermolabile analytes.

nodes.name: Mass Spectrometry
nodes.en: Studies the ionization, mass analysis, and fragmentation of molecules to determine molecular mass, elemental composition, and structural connectivity. Couples routinely with chromatographic front ends to deliver hyphenated identification and trace-level quantification across proteomics, metabolomics, forensics, and environmental analysis. Instrument and ionization-source innovation remains an active engineering frontier.
nodes.brief:
Studies the ionization, mass analysis, and fragmentation of molecules to determine molecular mass, elemental composition, and structural connectivity. Couples routinely with chromatographic front ends to deliver hyphenated identification and trace-level quantification across proteomics, metabolomics, forensics, and environmental analysis. Instrument and ionization-source innovation remains an active engineering frontier.

### Concepts
- **Ionization Techniques** — Convert neutral analytes into gas-phase ions via methods such as electrospray ionization, MALDI, or electron impact prior to mass analysis.
- **Mass Analyzers** — Separate ions by mass-to-charge ratio using quadrupole, time-of-flight, ion-trap, or Orbitrap technologies with differing resolution and speed.
- **Tandem Mass Spectrometry** — Performs sequential stages of mass selection and fragmentation to elucidate structure and achieve high-selectivity quantification (MS/MS).
- **Hyphenated LC-MS and GC-MS** — Couples chromatographic separation directly to mass spectrometric detection for combined resolving power and identification confidence.
- **High-Resolution and Accurate-Mass MS** — Determines elemental formulae from sub-ppm mass accuracy measurements, enabling non-targeted identification of unknowns.

nodes.name: Spectroscopic Methods
nodes.en: Examines the interaction of electromagnetic radiation with matter to identify and quantify chemical species from molecular vibrations, electronic transitions, and atomic emission or absorption. Spans UV-Vis, infrared, fluorescence, NMR, and atomic spectroscopies used across pharmaceutical, environmental, and materials analysis. Core theory is textbook-settled while instrument miniaturization and hyphenation continue to evolve.
nodes.cs: Zkoumá interakci elektromagnetického záření s látkou za účelem identifikace a kvantifikace chemických entit na základě molekulových vibrací, elektronových přechodů a atomové emise či absorpce. Zahrnuje UV-VIS, infračervenou, fluorescenční, NMR a atomovou spektroskopii používané ve farmaceutické, environmentální a materiálové analýze. Základní teorie je učebnicově ustálená, zatímco miniaturizace přístrojů a jejich spojování s dalšími technikami (hyphenation) se stále vyvíjí.
nodes.brief:
Examines the interaction of electromagnetic radiation with matter to identify and quantify chemical species from molecular vibrations, electronic transitions, and atomic emission or absorption. Spans UV-Vis, infrared, fluorescence, NMR, and atomic spectroscopies used across pharmaceutical, environmental, and materials analysis. Core theory is textbook-settled while instrument miniaturization and hyphenation continue to evolve.

### Concepts
- **UV-Visible Spectrophotometry** — Quantifies analytes from their absorption of ultraviolet and visible light, governed by the Beer-Lambert relationship.
- **Infrared and Raman Spectroscopy** — Probes molecular vibrational modes to identify functional groups and fingerprint compounds, including surface-enhanced Raman variants.
- **Atomic Absorption and Emission Spectroscopy** — Determines elemental composition from characteristic absorption or emission lines of atomized samples, including flame and graphite-furnace AAS.
- **Inductively Coupled Plasma Techniques** — Uses a high-temperature argon plasma to atomize and ionize samples for ultratrace elemental analysis by ICP-OES or ICP-MS.
- **Molecular Fluorescence Spectroscopy** — Exploits emission from excited fluorophores for highly sensitive detection of trace analytes and biomolecular labeling.
- **Nuclear Magnetic Resonance Spectroscopy** — Uses nuclear spin behavior in a magnetic field to determine molecular structure, purity, and quantitative composition.

nodes.name: Electroanalytical Chemistry
nodes.en: Investigates chemical information obtained through electrical signals — potential, current, charge, or impedance — generated at an electrode-solution interface. Includes potentiometry, voltammetry, and impedance-based methods central to clinical diagnostics, environmental monitoring, and battery research. Classical theory is well established, while nanostructured electrode materials remain an active development area.
nodes.cs: Zkoumá chemickou informaci získanou z elektrických signálů — potenciálu, proudu, náboje či impedance — vznikajících na rozhraní elektroda-roztok. Zahrnuje potenciometrii, voltametrii a impedanční metody klíčové pro klinickou diagnostiku, environmentální monitoring a výzkum baterií. Klasická teorie je dobře ustálená, zatímco nanostrukturované elektrodové materiály zůstávají aktivní oblastí vývoje.
nodes.brief:
Investigates chemical information obtained through electrical signals — potential, current, charge, or impedance — generated at an electrode-solution interface. Includes potentiometry, voltammetry, and impedance-based methods central to clinical diagnostics, environmental monitoring, and battery research. Classical theory is well established, while nanostructured electrode materials remain an active development area.

### Concepts
- **Potentiometry** — Measures equilibrium cell potential at zero current to determine ion activity, as in pH and ion-selective electrodes.
- **Voltammetry and Amperometry** — Records current as a function of applied potential to identify and quantify redox-active species, including cyclic and stripping variants.
- **Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy** — Applies small AC perturbations across a frequency range to characterize interfacial charge-transfer and mass-transport processes.
- **Coulometry** — Quantifies analyte amount from the total charge passed during a complete electrolytic conversion.
- **Ion-Selective and Membrane Electrodes** — Employ selective membranes to generate potentials proportional to the activity of a targeted ionic species.

nodes.name: Chemometrics and Data Analysis
nodes.en: Applies statistical, mathematical, and machine-learning methods to extract chemical information from complex, multivariate instrumental data. Covers calibration modeling, pattern recognition, and signal processing that underlie modern quantitative and qualitative analysis, especially with hyphenated and high-throughput instruments. A rapidly developing frontier as deep learning reshapes spectral and chromatographic data interpretation.
nodes.cs: Aplikuje statistické, matematické a strojově učené metody k extrakci chemické informace ze složitých, mnohorozměrných instrumentálních dat. Zahrnuje kalibrační modelování, rozpoznávání vzorů a zpracování signálu, na nichž stojí moderní kvantitativní i kvalitativní analýza, zejména u spojených a vysoce průchodných přístrojů. Rychle se rozvíjející oblast, kterou hluboké učení proměňuje interpretaci spektrálních a chromatografických dat.
nodes.brief:
Applies statistical, mathematical, and machine-learning methods to extract chemical information from complex, multivariate instrumental data. Covers calibration modeling, pattern recognition, and signal processing that underlie modern quantitative and qualitative analysis, especially with hyphenated and high-throughput instruments. A rapidly developing frontier as deep learning reshapes spectral and chromatographic data interpretation.

### Concepts
- **Multivariate Calibration** — Builds quantitative models such as partial least squares regression relating multivariate spectral data to analyte concentration.
- **Pattern Recognition and Classification** — Uses methods like principal component analysis and discriminant analysis to classify samples or detect outliers from complex datasets.
- **Design of Experiments** — Applies statistically structured experimental designs to optimize analytical methods with minimal, informative measurements.
- **Signal Processing and Deconvolution** — Removes noise, baseline drift, and overlapping-peak artifacts to recover accurate underlying analytical signals.
- **Machine Learning in Analytical Chemistry** — Applies neural networks and other learning algorithms to non-targeted identification, spectral interpretation, and predictive modeling.

nodes.name: Chemical Sensors and Biosensors
nodes.en: Develops devices that transduce a chemical or biological recognition event into a measurable signal for real-time, often field-deployable, analysis. Integrates recognition elements such as enzymes, antibodies, and aptamers with electrochemical, optical, or mass-based transducers. A fast-moving frontier driven by wearable, point-of-care, and environmental monitoring demand.
nodes.cs: Vyvíjí zařízení, která převádějí chemickou nebo biologickou rozpoznávací událost na měřitelný signál pro analýzu v reálném čase, často přímo v terénu. Integruje rozpoznávací prvky, jako jsou enzymy, protilátky a aptamery, s elektrochemickými, optickými nebo hmotnostními převodníky. Rychle se rozvíjející oblast poháněná poptávkou po nositelných zařízeních, point-of-care diagnostice a environmentálním monitoringu.
nodes.brief:
Develops devices that transduce a chemical or biological recognition event into a measurable signal for real-time, often field-deployable, analysis. Integrates recognition elements such as enzymes, antibodies, and aptamers with electrochemical, optical, or mass-based transducers. A fast-moving frontier driven by wearable, point-of-care, and environmental monitoring demand.

### Concepts
- **Electrochemical Biosensors** — Couple a biological recognition layer to an electrode transducer to generate a current or potential signal proportional to analyte concentration.
- **Optical Sensors** — Detect analytes via changes in absorbance, fluorescence, or surface plasmon resonance at a functionalized sensing surface.
- **Immunoassays and Aptasensors** — Exploit antibody-antigen or aptamer-target binding specificity to achieve highly selective molecular recognition.
- **Wearable and Point-of-Care Sensors** — Miniaturized sensing platforms enabling continuous or on-site chemical monitoring outside centralized laboratories.
- **Molecularly Imprinted Polymers** — Synthetic recognition materials templated to selectively rebind a target molecule, used as low-cost antibody substitutes.

nodes.name: Sample Preparation and Classical Wet Chemistry
nodes.en: Encompasses the extraction, cleanup, and preconcentration steps that render real-world samples compatible with instrumental analysis, alongside the classical gravimetric and titrimetric methods that remain reference techniques for accuracy. Critical for matrix simplification in complex food, environmental, and biological samples. Foundational theory is settled, while green and miniaturized extraction techniques continue to develop.
nodes.cs: Zahrnuje extrakci, čištění a prekoncentraci, které činí reálné vzorky kompatibilními s instrumentální analýzou, spolu s klasickými gravimetrickými a titrimetrickými metodami, jež zůstávají referenčními technikami pro přesnost stanovení. Klíčové pro zjednodušení matrice u složitých potravinových, environmentálních a biologických vzorků. Základní teorie je ustálená, zatímco šetrné a miniaturizované extrakční techniky se stále vyvíjejí.
nodes.brief:
